Stability ↔ bounded potential
examples: [WAVE8] Example instances will be attached as child DTUs when needed.
nextActions: Add child DTUs that instantiate this model with concrete parameters; add counterexample shadow DTUs.

math.implication: If ΔV≤-c||x||^2 outside neighborhood, trajectories enter neighborhood (stability).

steps: ok=true if constraints satisfied and ΔV non-increasing (or rule-specific).

meta.part: Part 7: Cognition, Psychology, Language, Culture, Institutions, Collective Intelligence (361–420)

human.summary: Institutions function as temporally extended control systems that persist across generations through formalized rules, cultural norms, and enforcement mechanisms. Unlike individual agents whose control horizons are bounded by lifespan, institutions maintain regulatory capacity over decades through structural inertia and institutional memory. Their stability derives from redundant control loops that survive individual component replacement.
bullets: Institutions outlive their creators by encoding control policies in structure rather than in individual agents
Institutional persistence requires memory mechanisms that survive complete personnel turnover
Control effectiveness degrades when institutional rules diverge from environmental constraints

definitions.definition: The resistance of an institution to state changes, proportional to its structural complexity and interdependent subsystems
The maximum temporal extent over which an institution can maintain effective regulatory action

invariants: ∀ institution I: lifespan(I) > max(lifespan(agent)) ⟹ ∃ memory M preserving control policy across turnover
rate(rule_update) must satisfy: rate(rule_update) < rate(env_change) × adaptation_coefficient for stability
∀ control loop L ∈ I: complete agent replacement preserves L within ε of original performance
claims: Institutions with more redundant control loops survive longer than those with single points of failure
The ratio of formal rules to informal norms predicts institutional rigidity and failure mode
Institutional memory fidelity decays exponentially with personnel turnovers without documentation
examples: The Roman Catholic Church maintaining doctrinal continuity across 2000 years despite complete generational replacement
Central banks preserving monetary policy frameworks across political regime changes through institutional independence

math.dynamics: I_{t+1} = I_t + α·ΔE_t - β·decay(I_t) + γ·repair(I_t, feedback_t)
math.implication: If V decreasing and bounded below, institution converges to environmentally adapted configuration

machine.primaryFormula: I_{t+1} = F(I_t, E_t, M_t) where M_t encodes institutional memory

meta.part: Part 7: Cognition, Psychology, Language, Culture, Institutions, Collective Intelligence (361–420)

human.summary: Institutional repair operates within existing constraint structures, adjusting parameters without altering the system's topology. Reform, by contrast, reconfigures the constraint graph itself — adding, removing, or rewiring control loops. The choice between repair and reform depends on whether observed failures stem from parameter drift or structural inadequacy.
bullets: Repair preserves institutional topology while adjusting parameters; reform alters the topology itself
Most institutional failures are misdiagnosed — parameter problems get reform solutions and vice versa
Successful reform requires a viable transition path through constraint space, not just a better endpoint

definitions.definition: Parameter adjustment within fixed structural topology to restore performance to design specifications
Reconfiguration of the institutional constraint graph, altering which control loops exist and how they interconnect

invariants: repair(I) preserves topology(I); reform(I) necessarily alters topology(I)
∀ reform path P: ∃ continuous trajectory through viable institutional states, else reform fails catastrophically
cost(repair) < cost(reform) when failure_source ∈ parameter_space, not structural_space
claims: Institutions that attempt reform for parameter-drift problems experience unnecessary disruption
Reform success probability correlates inversely with the number of simultaneous structural changes
Post-reform institutional performance follows a J-curve: initial degradation before improvement
examples: The New Deal as structural reform — adding entirely new regulatory agencies rather than adjusting existing ones
Toyota Production System as institutional repair — continuous parameter tuning within fixed manufacturing topology
nextActions: dtu_403, dtu_416

math.space: X = (P, T) where P ∈ R^p is parameter space and T ∈ G is topology space (graph of control loops)
math.dynamics: repair: P_{t+1} = P_t + η·∇performance; reform: T_{t+1} = mutate(T_t, failure_analysis)
constraints: viable(T_{t+1}) = true (reformed topology must be self-consistent)
math.implication: Optimal strategy minimizes V: repair when ∂V/∂P dominates, reform when ∂V/∂T dominates

meta.part: Part 7: Cognition, Psychology, Language, Culture, Institutions, Collective Intelligence (361–420)

human.summary: Institutional capture occurs when agents meant to be regulated by an institution gain sufficient influence to redirect its control policies toward their private interests. This represents a phase transition in the institution's attractor landscape — the system shifts from serving its designed purpose to serving the captor's objectives while maintaining the appearance of legitimate function.
bullets: Capture is a phase transition: the institution's attractor shifts from public-interest to private-interest basin
Captured institutions are more dangerous than absent ones because they provide legitimacy cover for extraction
Capture resistance requires structural separation between regulated agents and institutional control surfaces

definitions.definition: The process by which a regulatory body advances the commercial or political interests of the entities it is supposed to regulate
Structural properties of an institution that make it difficult for regulated entities to redirect control policies

invariants: capture_risk ∝ (information_asymmetry × access_to_control_surfaces) / accountability_mechanisms
∀ captured institution C: ∃ t_capture where objective(C, t > t_capture) ≈ objective(captor)
capture_detection requires: ∃ independent observer with access to both stated and actual institutional outputs
claims: Industries with higher information asymmetry between regulator and regulated experience faster capture
Rotating personnel between regulatory and industry roles accelerates capture via social network effects
Public transparency of institutional decision-making reduces capture velocity by increasing accountability surface
examples: The Federal Aviation Administration's relationship with Boeing prior to the 737 MAX crisis — shared personnel and deferred oversight
Academic journal capture by publishers — entities meant to disseminate knowledge instead restricting access for profit
nextActions: dtu_404, dtu_428

math.space: X = (policy_vector, influence_weights) ∈ R^n × R^m where policy_vector encodes institutional outputs
constraints: α_t ∈ [0,1] and dα/dt ≥ 0 without intervention (capture is monotonically increasing)
‖policy - mandate‖ ≤ detection_threshold (capture must remain below public detection)
math.implication: Capture proceeds until marginal extraction gain equals marginal trust loss: ∂V/∂α = 0

machine.primaryFormula: α_{t+1} = α_t + ε·(influence_t / resistance_t) where ε is capture rate constant

meta.part: Part 7: Cognition, Psychology, Language, Culture, Institutions, Collective Intelligence (361–420)

human.summary: Institutional latency accumulates as layers of procedure, precedent, and coordination overhead grow over time. Each individually rational addition to institutional process adds marginal delay, but the cumulative effect can render an institution unable to respond to environmental changes within relevant timescales. This latency debt compounds similarly to technical debt in software systems.
bullets: Every procedural addition is locally rational but globally contributes to institutional paralysis
Latency accumulation is invisible until environmental change rate exceeds institutional response rate
Latency reduction requires removing procedures, which faces stronger resistance than adding them

definitions.definition: The time delay between an environmental stimulus and the institution's completed response, including all procedural steps
The accumulated excess response time from procedural accretion, analogous to technical debt in software

invariants: dL/dt ≥ 0 in absence of deliberate pruning (latency monotonically increases without intervention)
institutional_viability requires: response_latency < environment_change_period
latency_removal_resistance > latency_addition_ease by factor proportional to procedure age
claims: Older institutions accumulate latency faster than younger ones due to precedent weight
Institutions facing existential threats temporarily reduce latency through crisis protocols, but revert afterward
The number of approval layers in a decision chain is the strongest predictor of institutional response time
examples: US federal procurement regulations taking 18+ months for routine purchases due to accumulated compliance layers
Hospital discharge processes averaging 4-6 hours due to accumulated documentation and approval requirements
nextActions: dtu_405, dtu_415

math.space: X = (procedures, latency, environment_rate) ∈ N × R+ × R+
math.implication: When L exceeds environmental change period, institutional decisions arrive after conditions have already shifted

machine.primaryFormula: viability = exp(-L_t / τ_environment) where τ_environment is environmental change timescale

meta.part: Part 7: Cognition, Psychology, Language, Culture, Institutions, Collective Intelligence (361–420)

human.summary: Institutional knowledge decays through personnel turnover, documentation obsolescence, and the gradual replacement of understanding with ritual compliance. The half-life of institutional knowledge depends on how it is encoded: tacit knowledge in individuals decays fastest, procedural knowledge in documents decays moderately, and structural knowledge embedded in architecture persists longest.
bullets: Knowledge encoded in people has the shortest half-life; knowledge embedded in structure has the longest
Ritual compliance replaces understanding when the reasons behind procedures are lost but the procedures remain
Documentation alone cannot prevent knowledge decay — it requires active reinterpretation by competent agents

definitions.definition: The time after which half of an institution's operational knowledge has been lost or become obsolete
Following institutional procedures without understanding their purpose, arising when explanatory context is lost

invariants: ∀ knowledge type K: half_life(tacit) < half_life(documented) < half_life(structural)
knowledge_decay_rate ∝ turnover_rate / documentation_quality
ritual_compliance_onset occurs when understanding_fraction < critical_threshold ≈ 0.3
claims: Institutions that lose more than 70% of senior personnel within 5 years experience critical knowledge loss
Video documentation preserves tacit knowledge 3-5x longer than text documentation alone
Institutions with apprenticeship models retain tacit knowledge longer than those relying solely on formal training
examples: NASA's loss of Saturn V manufacturing knowledge — complete documentation existed but tacit fabrication expertise was lost
Medieval cathedral building knowledge preserved through guild apprenticeships for centuries, lost when guilds dissolved
nextActions: dtu_414, dtu_416

math.space: X = (K_tacit, K_documented, K_structural) ∈ [0,1]³ representing knowledge retention fractions
math.dynamics: K_i(t+1) = K_i(t)·exp(-λ_i·turnover) + acquisition_i(t) for each knowledge type i
constraints: Σ K_i ≤ K_max (total knowledge capacity bounded)
math.potential: V(K) = Σ_i w_i·(1 - K_i)² weighted by operational criticality of each knowledge type
math.implication: Sustainable institutions maintain K above critical thresholds for all operationally essential knowledge types

machine.primaryFormula: K(t) = K_0·exp(-λt) + K_∞·(1 - exp(-μt)) balancing decay against ongoing acquisition

meta.part: Part 7: Cognition, Psychology, Language, Culture, Institutions, Collective Intelligence (361–420)

human.summary: Education As Constraint Transmission represents a fundamental principle in constraint-driven systems theory. When modeled as dynamics on a state manifold, the system reveals how education as constraint interact with as constraint transmission to produce emergent stable configurations. The key insight is that apparent complexity arises from simple constraint interactions operating across multiple scales.
bullets: The core mechanism of education as constraint transmission operates through constraint propagation across hierarchical levels
Stability in education as systems requires balancing exploration against constraint satisfaction
Observable constraint transmission patterns emerge from latent constraint geometry, not from designed intent

definitions.definition: The primary variable governing education as constraint transmission, measured as the deviation from constraint-satisfying equilibrium
The emergent property arising from education as dynamics under constraint pressure

invariants: ∀ system S exhibiting education: ∃ attractor A such that trajectories converge to A under constraint satisfaction
stability(education as constraint transmission) requires: Lyapunov derivative dV/dt ≤ -c·‖x - x*‖² for some c > 0
∀ perturbation δ: if ‖δ‖ < ε_critical, the system returns to equilibrium within time T ∝ log(1/‖δ‖)
claims: Systems with stronger education constraints exhibit faster convergence to stable configurations
The phase transition in education as occurs at a critical constraint density measurable in practice
Removing transmission constraints increases system entropy but may unlock novel attractor basins
examples: Biological evolution exhibiting education as constraint transmission through natural selection as a constraint-satisfaction process
Economic markets demonstrating education as constraint transmission through price equilibration under supply-demand constraints
nextActions: dtu_407, dtu_408

math.space: X ⊂ R^n where n captures the dimensionality of education parameters
math.dynamics: x_{t+1} = F(x_t, θ) where F encodes education as update rules
math.potential: V(x) = ‖x - x*‖²_W where x* is the constraint-optimal state and W encodes priority weighting
math.implication: If V non-increasing along trajectories, education as constraint transmission converges to constraint-satisfying equilibrium

machine.primaryFormula: x_{t+1} = Π_C[x_t + η·∇J(x_t)] where Π_C projects onto constraint set C
